data.title: Consecutive Weekly Declines And Fading Rallies
data.description:
Market Brief – Market Rebounds On Resolution Hopes

March closed as the worst quarter for the S&P 500 since 2022, with the index down roughly 7% on the quarter and every member of the Magnificent Seven finishing in the red. This past holiday-shortened trading week saw a sharp reflexive relief rally from the oversold conditions we discussed last week.

Monday opened with oil above $100 a barrel and sentiment fragile following the prior week’s correction-level losses. Markets churned with no conviction. Then, on Tuesday, reports surfaced that Iranian President Pezeshkian was open to ending the war, and markets exploded. The S&P surged 2.91%, the Dow added 1,125 points, and the Nasdaq rocketed 3.83%, its best single session since May. However, as we have noted previously, the “best trading days” tend to occur in the midst of the worst of times. Nonetheless, it was a legitimate relief rally, and for a day, it felt like the fog was lifting.

However, optimism was tempered on Wednesday, with the ADP employment topping expectations at 62,000 and retail sales surging by 0.6%. That news started the day out strong, but stronger data was overshadowed by inflationary pressures. President Trump’s prime-time address further reset the tone as he confirmed ceasefire talks were underway but said U.S. forces would “hit Iran hard” and send them “to the stone age” before withdrawing in two to three weeks. That wasn’t what markets were hoping for, and oil reversed from sub-$100 levels back toward $106 in overnight trading. With that, the markets opened lower on Thursday morning, wiping out the lion’s share of Wednesday’s gains, before rebounding to near breakeven on reports of Iran-Oman coordination to reopen the Hormuz Strait.

With the market closed for Good Friday, traders were left to stew over the weekend with the conflict still unresolved and a critical deadline looming. Nonetheless, as we will discuss below, the reflexive rally this week was not unexpected after 5 consecutive weeks of decline. With the markets decently oversold and clamoring for any piece of positive news to trade higher, the rally was not unexpected. However, we continue to suggest that investors remain cautious until the market rises above the 200-day moving average.

The one silver lining is valuation. As Morgan Stanley noted this past week, the S&P now trades roughly 17% cheaper than pre-war levels on forward earnings. That is approaching ranges historically associated with correction endings, provided the economy avoids recession and the Fed doesn’t hike. There is no guarantee of either, so caution remains a “trading position.”

Which brings us to the market.

Technical Backdrop – Market Breaks The 200-DMA

The S&P 500 closed Thursday at 6,566 ahead of the Good Friday holiday, snapping a brutal five-week losing streak with a gain of roughly 3% on the week. Tuesday’s +2.9% surge - the best session since May - was ignited by reports that Iran’s President is open to ending the war, combined with Trump’s announcement of “productive talks.” March still ended down over 5%, but the bounce off 6,300 produced a 4.2% rally from trough to Thursday’s close. The question heading into Q2 is whether this bounce has legs or is simply another dead-cat rally in a larger corrective phase.

As shown, the current rally is pushing into resistance at the 20-day moving average, which has crossed below the 200-day moving average. Notably, momentum has triggered a short-term buy signal, and relative strength is improving from very oversold levels. However, the big question is whether investors can retake the current resistance levels and push markets higher into next week, but the data suggests caution.

The internals tell us this bounce is real but fragile. As of Wednesday’s close, only 27.6% of S&P 500 constituents traded above their 50-day moving average, a percentile reading of just 12, meaning breadth has been this weak only 12% of the time. That is an extraordinarily narrow rally. Meanwhile, 49.2% remain above their 200-DMA (25th percentile), below average but notably above the sub-30% washout levels of the 2022 bear market. The RSI has recovered to 45.7 from the oversold low-30s in late March, and the McClellan Oscillator has turned positive after deeply negative readings. Both are constructive, but the percentage of stocks above the 50-DMA needs to expand well above 50% before we can call this anything more than a “reflexive bounce within a downtrend.“

So, is the market oversold enough for a sustained move higher? The short answer: the conditions are present for a tradeable rally, not a durable bottom. Seasonality helps as April is historically the second-best month for the S&P 500 (+1.4% avg per the Stock Trader’s Almanac), and earnings season kicks off with FactSet consensus at 13% YoY EPS growth. However, the macro overlay remains hostile: Brent near $117, the Fed has priced out cuts entirely (Macquarie expects a hike in 1H27), and the 10-year yield sits near 4.45%. The index closed Thursday still 1.2% below its 200-DMA (~6,642), and until price reclaims that level, the primary trend remains down. Investors who missed the bounce should not chase here.

Bottom line: If you are fully invested, this bounce is an opportunity to add hedges, not remove them. Consider put spreads on SPY or collar strategies on concentrated positions. If you’ve been building the shopping list we recommended, this isn’t the entry; that was the 6,300 level two weeks ago. The next entry comes on either a successful retest of March lows or a decisive close above the 200-DMA with breadth confirmation (50-DMA participation above 50%). Warren Buffett said it best Tuesday: he’d buy more Apple, “but not in this market.”

We agree. Defense over offense. Trade accordingly.

Key Catalysts Next Week

The first full week of Q2 is bookended by two events that will define the rate narrative for the next two months: the FOMC Minutes on Wednesday and March CPI on Friday. Everything else is secondary, other than what oil prices are doing.

The March 17–18 FOMC Minutes are the week’s first inflection point, but we already know the outcome. The Fed held rates steady at 3.50–3.75%, with only Miran dissenting in favor of a cut. However, the minutes will reveal how close the internal debate actually was. Given that the March meeting was the first to formally incorporate the Iran oil shock, the 15% global tariff regime, and the February payroll collapse into the Summary of Economic Projections, the minutes will be important to consider. In those projections, core inflation forecasts were revised higher to 2.7% for 2026, while GDP was upgraded to 2.4%. That combination, hotter inflation with resilient growth, justified the hold. But the question the markets need answered now is whether the spike in oil prices, which will eventually weigh on economic growth, changes that math.

Speaking of oil prices, Friday’s March CPI is the week’s anchor and arguably the most consequential inflation print of the year so far. February came in at +0.3% MoM headline and +2.4% YoY, with core at +0.3% / 2.8%. But March is the first month that fully captures the oil price surge toward $100 following the U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran. Energy-specific CPI rose 0.6% in February before the worst of the oil spike, which March will make materially worse. Food prices were already accelerating at +0.4% MoM. The core goods basket is where tariff passthrough resided, and RBC’s analysis flagged that declines in used-car prices had been masking the pressure in prior months. A hot March CPI could push rate cuts into December at the earliest, or off the table entirely. Any print above 0.4% MoM headline or 0.3% core will confirm those expectations.

Bottom line: The FOMC Minutes tell us what the Fed was thinking. The March CPI tells us whether they were right to hold. If inflation is accelerating while the labor market weakens, the policy trap is confirmed, and the market will have to price accordingly.

5-Consecutive Weekly Declines

In Tuesday’s Daily Market Commentary, we noted that the market had just experienced five consecutive weekly declines. That’s a lot, and as shown, it does happen, but not that often.

“The correction that we have seen so far has been quite normal, despite the more ‘doom and gloom’ narratives being espoused on the interwebs. Nonetheless, the recent 5-week streak of consecutive weekly declines is certainly worrisome. However, it isn’t unprecedented.”

As noted above, we did get a reflexive rally this past week, ending that streak of consecutive weekly declines. However, that latest spike in volatility left my inbox with questions piling up fast.

  • Is this the beginning of something worse?
  • Should you be selling? Buying? Standing still?

Let me give you the honest historical answer. I don’t know for certain, and historical data only gets you halfway to a useful decision.

Since 1965, the S&P 500 has recorded 26 separate instances of five or more consecutive weekly declines. That’s roughly once every 2.3 years, and these streaks feel catastrophic in real time. This is when investors make the most mistakes over time. The emotional stress of the decline, combined with “doomsayers,” drives investors to sell at the bottom. It is important to understand that, while these streaks feel alarming in real time, historical evidence suggests they function more as contrarian buy signals than as warnings of further collapse.

On average, the 4-week forward return following a fifth consecutive down week has been modestly positive, and the 12-month forward return has been meaningfully so. The data consistently shows that by the time the financial press is writing its most alarming prose, the market has already priced in a substantial portion of the bad news. That’s the mechanical reality of markets: they discount the future, often imperfectly and almost always uncomfortably early.

That pattern holds in both directions. The moments that feel like everything is in free-fall, as noted, have historically functioned more like contrarian buy signals than warnings of collapse. In March 2020, a five-week wipeout of roughly 34% was fully recovered by August, and the index was 16% above its pre-COVID peak by year-end. After the October 1987 crash, 12-month forward returns were emphatically positive, despite the four-week forward return looking like a catastrophe.

The Reflexive Rally Was Not Surprising

The market rallied on Tuesday and Wednesday, with Tuesday’s rally one of the best trading days since 2022. However, that should also be unsurprising, since the best trading days tend to cluster with the worst market periods. As we noted in Stock Market Breadth on Monday:

“The single most damaging decision most investors make during periods of falling stock market breadth is selling. The data on this is unambiguous. Seven of the market’s 10 best days in any given 20-year period occur within two weeks of the 10 worst days, according to JPMorgan Asset Management research. The best days follow the worst days because fear-driven selling creates dislocations that are rapidly corrected. You can see this in the chart below, that the best and worst days are clustered together.”

In other words, while investors are always told to just “buy and hold” because they will miss the 10 best days if they don’t, investors should focus on mitigating the risk of significant capital losses during those periods.

This doesn’t mean you can effectively miss all the bad days; however, given that higher-volatility periods tend to cluster, understanding when to reduce exposure can significantly improve outcomes over time. Even if you miss the 10 best days along the way. That math applies with particular force in setups like the current one. Since 1974, according to data compiled by Clear Perspective Advisors, the S&P 500 has returned more than 24% on average following a market correction. Only 25% of the 48 corrections since World War II have progressed into full bear markets. In other words, there is a 75% chance this correction will not turn into a bear market. However, dismissing that 25% entirely is just as foolish for future outcomes.

This is why the rally this past week was not unexpected. Oversold conditions, exhausted sellers, aggressive short positioning, and algorithmic covering all tend to converge after sustained selling pressure. Goldman’s trading desk noted this week that the capitulation checklist is nearly complete, with the S&P now below all key moving averages and below critical CTA selling thresholds. When those conditions are clear, the snap-back can be sharp. But it’s a trap.

Why do I say that? Because that is what I have learned repeatedly over 35 years of managing money. The rallies that come off oversold extremes are seductive precisely because they feel like confirmation that the worst is over. They’re fast, they’re loud, and they draw in sidelined capital chasing performance. Sentiment indicators flip from extreme fear to cautious optimism in a matter of days.

Bottom line: If the bull case for this rally is ‘stocks were down a lot, and people were scared,’ that’s not a fundamental argument. It’s a positioning argument. It expires quickly.

And in the current environment, the macro headwinds haven’t gone anywhere. Even if the Iranian conflict is resolved on Monday, private credit stress remains, the impact of higher oil and gasoline prices is working its way through the economy, and questions remain about artificial intelligence.

But there is another reason to fade this rally.

Earnings Hit Still Coming

The difference between a durable recovery and a dead-cat bounce is almost always visible in the underlying fundamentals, not the price action alone. Right now, the fundamentals argue for caution.

Goldman’s own scenario analysis puts a moderate slowdown path at 6,300 on the S&P 500 and a severe oil-shock path as low as 5,400. Neither of those scenarios is priced into current earnings estimates. S&P 500 companies are still being modeled at roughly $309 per share in earnings for 2026, figures built on assumptions about GDP growth and energy costs that the past eight weeks have materially challenged. When earnings revisions begin in earnest, they tend to hit in waves. We’re likely in the early innings of that process, and it will impact forward returns. The reason is that the market trades off forward earnings expectations; if those expectations fall, the market reprices for lower earnings growth.

Add to that the technical damage. Breaking below the 200-day moving average is not a minor event. Historically, a clean break below that level without a swift recapture has resolved to the downside more often than not. The index now sits below all key moving averages, and the burden of proof has shifted. Bulls need to prove the trend has reversed. Sellers don’t need to prove anything.

“As shown in the comparative table below, understanding the difference between a sustained break of the 200-dma and one that wasn’t was critical to future returns.” – Break Of The 200-DMA

We are still within the first 4 weeks of the break of the 200-day moving average. The market rally this past week, following those five consecutive weekly declines, doesn’t mean the downside risk is over. If the market fails to climb above that now-critical resistance level, the potential for a retest of recent lows increases.

However, this doesn’t mean you get out of the markets entirely.

So, When Should You Start Accumulating

The one thing that bothers me most about the “Perpetual Purveyors of Doom” is that they repeatedly tell you for years that the market is going to crash. Eventually, they will be correct. However, what they don’t tell you is when to start buying the cataclysm. The voices are currently louder than ever.

However, the current market backdrop is nothing like the catastrophic events of the past, such as the financial crisis or the dot-com crash. This is a well-needed correction after the massive post-“Liberation Day” rally last summer. Nonetheless, the damage done during declines is always troublesome, but it needs to be kept in perspective.

Yes, we certainly suggest using this rally to cash in and reduce risk. After consecutive weekly declines, a rally was inevitable. However, I am also not saying “sell everything” or “stay in cash indefinitely.” The market will eventually bottom and recover. The reason is that the market will eventually “price in” the risk and begin to look forward. The economy will adapt and begin to grow. As such, the question isn’t whether to own equities, it’s just a question of when and at what price.

There are four specific conditions I want to see before moving from a defensive to a constructive stance. None of them requires perfect clarity. All of them require meaningful evidence.

None of these conditions exists today. They may develop over the coming weeks or months. When they do, I’ll tell you. However, here is how to position for what is likely coming next.

Investor Tactics For What Comes Next

Following five consecutive weekly declines, the market’s bounce this week could continue for a bit longer. This isn’t rocket science, and is something we repeat often. It is just a process to manage near-term risk.

  • Treat any near-term rally as an opportunity to rebalance, not to add exposure. Use strength to trim positions outside your target allocation and to reduce concentration in sectors most exposed to energy-cost pressure - consumer discretionary, industrials, and highly leveraged names.
  • Raise cash to a level that lets you sleep at night and act when opportunities arrive. That number is different for every investor, but the point is intentional: cash is a position, not a failure of nerve. Having it means you can be opportunistic when others are forced to sell.
  • Hedge risk that you want to keep. If you hold long-term positions, consider hedging them to reduce portfolio volatility.
  • Watch the 200-DMA retake attempt closely. A failed retake - where the market rallies back toward that level and then rolls over - is one of the clearest signals that the intermediate-term trend remains down. A successful retake on expanding volume materially changes the picture.
  • Stress-test your portfolio for oil above $100 through year-end. Goldman’s bear case is 5,400 on the S&P. That’s a decline from current levels that would test the tolerance of most retail investors. Know your number before the market finds it for you.
  • Don’t abandon fixed income. Duration has been painful, but investment-grade credit and short-term Treasuries are doing exactly what they should: providing ballast. A barbell approach - short-duration credit on one side, selectively opportunistic equity exposure on the other - remains the structure most likely to survive what comes next.

Again, this is nothing new, and we can sum it all up in just five words:

Defense over offense. Trade accordingly.

Market Statistics & Analysis

Weekly technical overview across key sectors, risk indicators, and market internals

Market & Sector X-Ray: Market Cracks Support

As noted last week: “With every other sector extremely oversold, the logical setup now is for that rotation out of energy into other areas of the market to begin as soon as clarity on Iran emerges.” As shown in the upper right box, that is what happened this past week. However, with no real clarity on a resolution for Iran, the current rotation will likely be short-lived. The relief rally this past week is an opportunity to reduce risk and rebalance portfolios for now.

Technical Composite: 39.13 – Relief Rally From Deep Oversold

As stated last week, “The odds of a reflexive rally are increasing. We are currently at levels we haven’t seen since 2022.” That reflexive rally came last week, and improved technical conditions. However, if this is a bounce within a corrective cycle, it probably won’t last long.

Fear/Greed Index: 53.23 – Investors Remain Cautious

This past week, sentiment stabilized as the market bounced from deeply oversold levels. As we noted last week, “The Iran conflict continues, and energy prices remain elevated, pushing investors to become increasingly cautious. While not at ‘fear levels’ yet, the selloff has been rather sharp, so a reflexive rally is likely before a further decline.“ The reflexive rally is your opportunity to rebalance risk.

Relative Sector Performance

As noted last week, “Given the divergence between energy and the rest of the market, profit-taking in energy seems prudent.” This past week, energy sold off while the rest of the market rallied. Energy remains overbought, although in a better position, and Discretionary, Financials, and Healthcare are the most oversold.

MFBR Index (Money Flow/Breadth Ratio Indicator): 35% = Risk Off

NEW! MFBR Index: The Money Flow Breadth Ratio (MFBR) model is a rules-based equity allocation framework that uses weekly S&P 500 money flow data to generate buy, sell, and neutral signals. It is designed to systematically adjust portfolio equity exposure in response to the direction and persistence of institutional capital flows, aiming to reduce drawdowns while capturing the majority of market upside.

As of the most recent weekly reading, the MFBR stands at 35% and falling, placing it in the 35–40% zone - the single worst sub-range within SELL territory. A 25-year backtest covering 1,351 weekly observations from January 2000 through March 2025 reveals just how unfavorable this zone has been historically. Across all 110 weeks where the MFBR registered below 40%, the average forward return was +0.3% at one month, -0.1% at three months, and +1.8% at six months - well below the all-period baseline of +0.6%, +1.8%, and +3.7% over those same horizons. Win rates tell an even starker story: only 53.2% of SELL periods saw positive returns at one month and just 46.8% at six months, compared to the baseline win rate of 69.5% at six months.

Sector Model & Risk Ranges

Both the dollar and energy remain well outside their respective risk ranges, suggesting that reversals are likely if there are any short-term resolutions in Iran. Outside of those two sectors, the massive deviations we saw in other sectors have all been reversed, which is why we repeatedly warned to take profits in those areas. The probability of a rather strong reflexive rally is building, particularly as we move into April.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Brighthouse Financial: A Deal-Driven Opportunity, Not A Long-Term Compounder
data.description:
Introduction

In my view, Brighthouse Financial (BHF) is currently transforming from a problematic value stock to the object of a merger and acquisition transaction when, in February, shareholders officially confirmed the $4.1 billion merger with Aquarian Capital. Even though the deal is forecasting a significant premium at a current share price of around $60, the price-to-book value is still at around 0.5x, without the accumulated other comprehensive income, which at the end of 2025 recorded around $154 level.

This gap between the current market price of around $60 and the agreed transaction value, which is $70 per share, implies around 15% upside but also shows that investors are cautious due to the regulatory approvals and potential enforcement risks. Fiscal year 2025 results showed a deep gap between operating capacity and net profitability: adjusted EPS reached $16, though GAAP net income due to volatility of hedging instruments was just $5.7.

The record $10.3 billion in annuity sales in 2025 shows a strong market position, though the 456% RBC ratio and $0.9 billion in liquid asset holdings level are critical stability factors while waiting for the transaction to close. The company is now not focusing on the historical share buyback strategy, which lowered share count to 57.2 million, but on the arbitrage opportunity, which is limited by the increasing corporate costs, which in Q4 rose to $234 million. Also in 2025, there was insider selling by the management at around the $60 share price range, which suggests that the market currently doesn't have much confidence in a higher organic share price growth without the completion of the transaction.

Business Overview

Brighthouse Financial operates as one of the biggest US annuity and life insurance providers, whose business model in 2026 is defined by a shift to less capital-intensive products. The company, which in 2017 separated from MetLife, has consistently been doing a product rotation, lowering the old, variable annuities with high-guarantee products from the portfolio and increasing the weight of shield-level indexed annuities.

Annuity sales at the end of 2025 reached record numbers at $10.3 billion, which directly added to the RBC ratio, reaching 456%, which is exceeding the company's set target interval of 400-450%. This rotation allows the company to optimize the balance sheet and decrease sensitivity to market fluctuations, because the new generation products require lower reserves than historical liabilities.

The stock is traded at around 10.4x P/E, based on the earnings per share of $5.70. Even though the company earned $331 million in net income in 2025, its valuation remains low, with a P/B ratio of 0.5x, compared to around $154 book value per share without AOCI, which better reflects the underlying capital position without unrealized losses from interest rate movements. Such a discount shows investors caution about the impact of obligations and interest rates in the run-off segment. An important factor in 2026 is the confirmed merger with Aquarian Capital for $70 per share, which is valued as an attempt to stabilize the capital structure in the private format. In the health insurance segment, SmartCare sales also reached record numbers at $143 million, though annuities remain the dominating part of revenues, generating the biggest part in tax revenue and ensuring $0.9 billion in liquid assets at the holding level.

Valuation

Brighthouse Financial shares in the market are valued at a clear discount compared to the sector's averages, despite the confirmed merger agreement. With the 2025 Q4 data, the company's book value is at 0.5x, and valued based on the adjusted book value without AOCI, which makes up $154, the ratio drops to around 0.4x.

This is well below the direct competitors, such as Lincoln National, which is at 0.6x P/B, or Prudential Financial at 0.8x ratio. The low P/B ratio indicates that the market is still skeptical about BHF's asset value and long-term obligations related to old annuity portfolios and volatility risk.

The forward P/E ratio is at 3.7x, which is lower than the five-year average of around 4.3x. Even though Aquarian's offer of $70 per share should act as a price anchor, the current share price is only in the $60 range. This 14% arbitrage difference reflects the regulatory risks and investors doubts regarding the terms of completion of the transaction. The financial health of the company is negatively impacted by the debt-to-equity ratio, which is reaching 1.3x and is above the critical level of 1.0x in the insurance sector. While the preliminary figure of RBC is at 456%, a high leverage and a fixed net profit drop to $331 million confirm the volatility of operating results. Such a capital structure increases sensitivity to market fluctuations; therefore, the discount compared to competitors is justified by the fundamental risk factors, not market inefficiency.

Risks

Brighthouse Financial's business model remains strongly dependent on macroeconomic factors, which directly impact the company's balance sheet and profitability. The main risk arises from the variable annuities segment, which makes up the biggest part of the company's obligations and is very sensitive to stock market fluctuations and interest rate changes.

The 2025 Q4 report showed that costs that are related to adverse changes in market risk benefits grew more than 300%, which caused the net income to drop to $112 million, compared to $646 million last year. This jump suggests that the company's hedging strategies don't fully compensate for fast drops in the market, while their costs directly lower operating margins.

Also, the company's investment portfolio, over $120 million, is exposed to credit risk; any company's bond rating downgrade would require additional capital reserves, which would limit free cash flows. The current P/B of 0.50 confirms that the market values the company's assets with a 50% discount, fearing the fulfillment of long-term commitments and possible write-offs. Additional uncertainty is added with the unfinished Aquarian Capital acquisition deal of $70 per share. Even though shareholders agreed with the deal, regulators are still assessing the impact on policyholder security, and the deal's collapse could make the share price drop significantly.

Thesis Summary

In my view, Brighthouse Financial today is not a classic value investment; rather, it is an event-driven situation, which depends on one clear catalyst, the Aquarian Capital transaction completion. A current 0.5x P/B and a low forward P/E at first glance may look like a deep undervaluation, though this discount is not random. The market clearly discounts the structural risks: complex annuity portfolio, hedging inefficiency in stress situations, and the high balance sheet sensitivity to interest rates and market fluctuations.

An arbitrage possibility exists to get around 14% upside to $70 per share, though it is not free money. It is limited by regulatory risks, a weak capital structure, and the fact that even the management is selling their shares, which shows a limited belief in organic upside without the deal. If the deal closes, it becomes quite a clean, event-driven return. If not, the market will quickly go back to fundamentals, where earnings volatility and complex balance sheets could put even further pressure on the valuation.

If the transaction is finished, the shareholder will lock in a defined return, though further growth potential is limited because the $70 per share offer caps valuation. Though if the transaction fails, the stock will most likely be reevaluated by the fundamental indicators, where revenue volatility and a complex balance sheet structure could justify a much lower valuation.

That is why I view BHF not as a long-term compounder, but rather as a risky but structured arbitrage trade, where returns depend not on the business quality but on the outcome of the deal.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: My Faith In The Value And Upside Is Unshaken - Rating Upgrade
data.description:
Background and Thesis

I first covered Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) back in June of 2025 and came away with a hold rating owing to an expensive valuation. In October, I upgraded to a buy after SFM suffered a long slide, despite the company generally doing well. Unfortunately, I got on the train a few stations too soon, and the stock suffered a sell-off after a quarterly earnings report left investors unsatisfied.

I haven’t lost faith in SFM, however. In fact, with shares selling for less than $80, I believe it’s one of the better values on the stock market, and as I’ll outline below, Sprouts is well-positioned amid this admittedly rather treacherous economy and tough-to-decipher market conditions. Yet Sprout’s emphasis on value, including through private labels, in combination with a growth strategy that emphasizes well-to-do suburbs that aren’t already crowded, has me feeling good about SFM’s future. Let's take a closer look.

Why Sprouts is a Good Bet Amid This Economy

While market valuations across the board have cooled off a bit in recent weeks, the market overall remains pretty pricey by historical standards, as we see with the Shiller CAPE ratio. It can be hard to find good deals in regard to PE and PS ratios, but SFM is looking pretty good with a 14.29 GAAP PE ratio (TTM) and a 0.84 PS ratio (TTM). I’ll look at the valuation again in the next section for now but wanted to provide that data as it’s a sort of foundation for my other points.

Sprouts has also been enjoying strong growth, with the revenue growth (YOY) topping 14%, compared to a sector median for consumer staples of just ~2%. What I especially like with the growth is SFM’s growth strategy, which is currently focused on suburbs and edge exurban areas. Many of these areas are growing and are generally affluent. Given the K-shaped economy and the substantial risk of sustained inflation this year, targeting more affluent customers strikes me as the way to go. Sprouts is also starting to enter the Midwest and key East Coast markets, like New York. A quick glance at the map below shows that Sprouts has a good amount of room to grow.

Sprouts is also emphasizing smaller stores, with a footprint between 20,000 and 25,000 square feet. Often, the company targets areas where there isn’t as much competition from larger organic-premium stores (like Whole Foods). In a sense, SFM is adopting an expansion strategy similar to dollar stores. While dollar stores often look for smaller rural and suburban areas not already dominated by nearby big box stores, Sprouts looks for communities not already stuffed with Whole Foods and the like.

While Sprouts competes more against stores like Whole Foods than, say, Walmart (WMT), it still emphasizes value. We can think of Sprouts stores as value-premium stores, which I believe is the right position to be in amid the K-shaped economy. The company is also focused on targeting younger customers, who often are more price-conscious but also more resistant to economic downturns.

Crucially, Sprouts is emphasizing private label products, which I’m a huge fan of. Amid high fuel prices, inflation, economic uncertainty, and high overall costs of living, private labels are where I want to be when it comes to investing in retailers. Private labels offer competitive pricing, and companies like Sprouts also emphasize quality, organics, etc. As of Q3 2025, private labels accounted for more than 25% of sales.

Sprouts also offers a variety of pre-prepped meals that customers can take home to heat up for dinner. This is another area that strikes me as smart. When consumers come under pressure, they often skip eating out. Pre-prepped meals negate much of the inconvenience of preparing food at home while still offering strong value.

Sprouts is also using targeted couponing and deals. Rather than sending out generic coupons, SFM is emphasizing analyzing the customer’s diet (e.g., keto, vegetarian) and then offering deals and coupons based on that diet. Consumers under financial pressure often turn to coupons and deals to save money.

SFM Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

My opinions often align with or are at least close to Seeking Alpha’s quant tools, but with SFM we’re pretty far apart. After the long slide in share prices, I can see why the quant tools are negative on SFM. However, I believe when you dig deeper, a buy story quickly emerges. Take the valuation score, which the quant tools assign a C- grade. Still, when you look at the valuation page, SFM gets strong marks across the board, as shown below.

As already mentioned, SFM offers attractive PE and PE ratios, especially given that this is still a fast-growing company. Individually, these metrics get strong grades. In fact, SFM gets dinged only for price/book. SFM does have a fair bit of liabilities on its books, with current liabilities totalling $870.8 million and total liabilities weighing in at $2.756 billion, while cash and short-term investments total just $257.3 million. A stronger balance sheet would be nice, but nothing here worries me.

Profitability here is interesting as well, with the net income margin weighing in at 5.95%. This is substantially higher than Walmart and Kroger (KR), which respectively enjoy net income margins of just ~3% and .69%. High profitability in and of itself, for consumer staples, is nice, but just as importantly, there also likely is room for SFM to run more aggressive discounting should the larger economy deem it necessary. Of course, if profitability takes a hit, it’ll be priced into the stocks, but the wiggle room does, to some extent, mitigate risks.

Growth is strong as well, with revenues up 14% YOY. Growth is partially driven by new store openings, with 37 stores opened in 2025 (shown below). Yet comparable store sales were up more than 7%, which is especially appealing. Walmart’s comparable store sales were up “only” 4.6%, a healthy number, to be sure, but not as strong as SFM.

All in all, I believe there is a solid growth story with Sprouts. I also like their plans in terms of customers and markets to target. I’ve mentioned this already, but I think it’s important enough to repeat: targeting more affluent areas and customers in this particular economy is the right move in my opinion.

Why I Could Be Wrong

First, while Sprouts does emphasize value in the premium space, it’s still in the premium space. It’ll be hard to outvalue companies like Walmart. In recent quarters, Walmart has actually secured most of its growth from more affluent customers, many of whom are likely substituting groceries for eating out or otherwise downgrading from more premium brands and stores. This is a threat that can’t be overlooked, but I do believe Sprouts' emphasis on value and private labels will help.

Then there’s the elephant in the grocery store: Amazon, which owns Whole Foods. Amazon has been emphasizing private labels, both Amazon- and Whole Foods-affiliated. Its massive size will also afford economies of scale. While I believe there is still room in the market for companies like Sprouts, the Amazon threat should not be overlooked.

Key Takeaway: Sprouts Offers Value in-store and in the Market Too

I believe there’s a fair bit of value here. Sprouts is still growing at a solid pace, and its growth strategy strikes me as the right moves in this market. The private label emphasis is especially attractive, and I find the custom coupons/deals quite interesting. Small stores focusing on markets with less competition should insulate SFM to some extent from tough competition. With shares right now below $80, the value is hard to pass up.

data.title: CG Oncology: Passing A Red Line For What I Would Call A Reasonable Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

When I last took a look at CG Oncology (CGON), I reaffirmed a cautious "Buy" sentiment even though the stock had been on the rise for some time. Since then, the stock has continued to climb in value ever higher, to many billions in market cap (at the time of writing). Today, I want to digest what we've seen so far for 2026 with CGON's clinical efforts, and give my thoughts as to why I think a decline in stock price is more than likely, despite the positive news the company has shared over the past year and the upcoming catalysts they've signaled.

Pipeline Updates

Cretostimogene Grenadenorepvec

Cretostimogene is CGON's entry into the emerging field of oncolytic virus therapy, which entails delivering viruses designed to replicate only in tumor cells, ultimately targeting them for direct killing by the viral infection and also for an adaptive immune response. The target has not changed; CGON is pursuing approval for cretostimogene in patients with non-metastatic bladder cancer, with updates over 2025 focusing on findings from the single-arm, phase 3 BOND-003 trial, including a cohort of patients in BCG-unresponsive, non-muscle invasive bladder cancer with carcinoma in situ showcasing encouraging complete response rates. More findings from this cohort are anticipated in 2026, which will add to what we know about the durability of response to treatment. CGON anticipates submitting a BLA to seek approval in this group of patients later this year.

Arguably, the next major catalyst for CGON will be the presentation of topline data from a pivotal trial of cretostimogene after resection in intermediate-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer, PIVOT-006. This brings a readout one year ahead of schedule for the first randomized registrational study in this risk category.

CGON is also continuing to work on potential combinations using cretostimogene, with findings from a phase 2 study, CORE-008, expected in the first half of 2026 detailing the use of the virus in combination with gemcitabine for patients with high-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer.

Financial Overview

At the end of 2025, CGON held $32.5 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $709.7 million in marketable securities. They had $3.2 million in commercial and development revenue. Operating expenses were $194.8 million. After interest income, the net loss for the year was $161 million. Net cash used for operations was $132.3 million, up from $78.7 million in 2024.

At this annual cash burn rate, CGON has between 5 and 6 years of cash on hand, not taking into account the inevitable rise in development costs as they continue to advance their pipeline deeper into clinical trials. This cash balance does not take into account the fundraising efforts CGON has made through early 2026, raising another $188 million, and the company expects that the cash runway will be into the first half of 2029.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Attacking on multiple fronts with late-stage trial data

We've seen the presentation of some key data from CGON's late-stage clinical trials already, and the company is continuing to guide further important readouts, including one in intermediate-risk bladder cancer that could be a game changer for the field (PIVOT-006). This will be very important to continue to watch as the first half of 2026 develops.

Strength - An approval is likely

Beyond that, with the plan to push cretostimogene toward approval in a treatment setting similar to ImmunityBio's Anktiva, CGON is rapidly evolving itself toward becoming a commercial enterprise, which could serve as the springboard for further development of their agents and eventual profitability. Although we don't have a randomized trial for the BCG-unresponsive high-risk setting, there is still a strong likelihood that the findings from BOND-003 will be good enough to justify a provisional approval, CGON emphasizes that BOND-003 is designed to be compliant with 2018 FDA guidance.

We've seen some waffling from the FDA on whether these precedents hold up over time, but there is a strong charge to get new agents approved, and the findings from BOND-003 look at least as good as what we saw from Anktiva in a similar setting.

Strength - Cash position poses no risk at this time

Beyond that, CGON has far more than enough cash to reach commercialization, even if costs ballooned substantially and there was a delay with the FDA submission or review. You can easily count their cash runway in "years," and that's not a bad place to be for the investment thesis at this time.

Risk - Market valuation puts CGON in uncomfortable company

All that said, I have deep reservations about biotech stocks that gain a lot of hype on anticipated news, with the first big surge of 2026 coinciding with the announcement that PIVOT-006 will likely read out ahead of schedule. That is indeed good news, but we don't know the results of this trial, and reading out early does not necessarily mean that the trial will read out positive.

As I write this, CGON's market cap is $5.7 billion, putting it in league with ImmunityBio, a company with multiple years of sales and a very enthusiastic retail investor base that has many believers in the wide-ranging applicability of Anktiva.

Compare that with Urogen, a company with a much more narrow approval and a commercial pipeline that keeps them grounded in bladder cancer: market cap of around $1 billion. CGON is positioning itself to get approvals similar to what Anktiva received, possibly with the carcinoma in situ component added, and intermediate-risk bladder cancer could soon be on the table, as well.

The question is this, though: do those indications justify being valued alongside ImmunityBio? Time will tell, and I cannot make a clear value judgment while we don't have any real business to speak of yet (i.e., no sales or approvals yet). But this kind of enthusiasm from the market creates a lot of risk of substantial correction in the event of any negative catalyst. That could be a perceived delay in filing the BLA (we have not been given many specifics on this timeline), any pushback from the FDA, or a negative trial readout.

Bottom-Line Summary

The CGON pipeline is exciting for its potential and near-term applicability in the space of bladder cancer, and by all indications, they seem to be on the precipice of getting a first approval. However, the current valuation gives me a lot of concern, since the road is rarely smooth. While the news being anticipated for CGON is exciting, it also presents the opportunity for disappointment, which could be a massive negative catalyst from this near-$6 billion valuation.

And when keeping in mind that non-muscle invasive bladder cancer has a total valuation of around $4 billion as of 2024, it makes it more and more difficult to see that the near-term picture for CGON stock is going to be further appreciation. They're currently being valued as if they're already generating hundreds of millions in sales of cretostimogene, which I don't feel is quite justified when you take into account that their peer, IBRX, had revenues of only $113 million in 2025. We're a long way off from CGON having a chance to make their case to the market, and that's assuming everything with approval and launch goes smoothly.

It's an odd position to find yourself in as an analyst here. I like pretty much everything I see with CGON, but the market likes it a lot more than I do, and they've ascribed a valuation I do not think is going to hold up indefinitely, not until they're deep in the proving stage for an approved product. Before that, I expect we'll see a much more favorable entry point. I am switching my sentiment to a "sell" based on this, despite the news we've seen.

data.title: Netflix: Three Reasons To Expect An Earnings Beat
data.description:
Introduction

Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) is the world’s leading streaming platform with over 325 million paid subscribers across more than 190 countries. The company primarily derives its revenue through subscriptions with three pricing tiers (ad-supported, Standard, and Premium). This subscription revenue is complemented by a rapidly growing advertising business that is becoming a meaningful contributor to overall revenue.

Despite macroeconomic headwinds from tariffs and geopolitical turmoil pressuring the consumer, Netflix continues to deliver a value proposition that is difficult for its customer base to relinquish. However, the market acknowledges this risk and has punished the stock in anticipation of weaker performance, with shares down 26% from ATH.

With 1Q26 earnings on April 16th, 2026, I believe Netflix’s recent share price drop creates an attractive entry point for investors. In addition, I see three specific tailwinds that will assist Netflix in delivering an earnings beat and full-year guidance raise: a recently announced price increase, accelerating advertising revenue supported by an in-house ad tech stack, and a $2.8 billion breakup fee. As a result, I rate NFLX a Buy.

Business Overview

Netflix derives its revenue from two primary streams: subscriptions and advertising. While there is not a segment breakdown, there is clear commentary from management. In 2025, advertising revenue grew 2.5x to approximately $1.5 billion. For 2026, management has guided to a rough doubling of that figure to approximately $3 billion.

As per Netflix Co-CEO Gregory K. Peters on their 4Q25 Earnings Call:

Ad sales, 2.5x in 2025. We expect that business to roughly double again in 2026 to about $3 billion... So we're making good progress and the opportunity ahead of us is massive. We are still under 10% of TV time in all major markets which we compete. We've got hundreds of millions of households around the world still to sign up. We're just about 7% of the addressable market in terms of consumer and ad spend. "

While their ad revenue business was only created 3 years ago, it is quickly becoming a meaningful contributor to total revenue. Today, ad revenue represents 3.3% of revenue ($1.5 billion of $45.2 billion in 2025). At the guided $3 billion run rate against an estimated $51.2 billion in 2026 revenue, ad revenues would represent 5.9% of total revenue. In addition, NFLX has built its own proprietary ad infrastructure, which launched in late 2025, reducing dependency on other companies.

By owning the full technology stack, Netflix commands direct control over inventory pricing, targeting capabilities, and margins, improving unit economics as the business continues to scale.

Churn

On March 26, 2026, NFLX announced a price increase: ad-supported will be increased by $1, with standard and premium increasing by $2. A common concern against NFLX’s price increases is churn: the expectation that price increases will result in a permanent decrease in subscribers. Fortunately, the data for Netflix suggests otherwise.

According to third-party research from Antenna, as many as 44% of churned Netflix subscribers resubscribe within 12 months. This highlights NFLX’s platform stickiness and their ability to raise prices as needed.

This is also highlighted by their value proposition. As per Moffett Nathanson’s equity research, Netflix delivers the lowest cost per hour of entertainment relative to other major streaming platforms. According to their research, NFLX’s revenue per hour is only 48 cents, relative to 64-93 cents per hour for Disney+, Peacock, HBO Max, and Paramount+. This assessment implies that Netflix provides the cheapest hour of entertainment in streaming.

This has allowed NFLX to raise their subscription prices at a low-to-mid single-digit CAGR in the past 15 years. As per Co-CEO Gregory Peters, he sees ample opportunity in the future for further price increases.

Finally, we have to recognize that subscriber churn is not necessarily a win or lose scenario. A subscriber downgrading from the Premium Plan ($26.99) to the ad-supported tier ($8.99) does not represent a complete loss to NFLX. They still remain in the NFLX ecosystem and continue to generate revenue due to ad sales. As per Antenna, there is also a strong chance of said consumer re-upgrading to the Premium Plan.

ARPU

To illustrate the opportunity from NFLX’s 2026 price increases, I’ve constructed a baseline using NFLX’s reported blended U.S. ARPU of $17.26. NFLX last reported this number in their 2024 8-K.

While NFLX does not disclose U.S. subscriber tier composition, I reverse engineered a blended ARPU that is similar to their reported figure to establish a baseline. This composition is 40% ad-supported, 30% standard, and 30% Premium. In addition, I modelled an expected churn of 2%.

This results in approximately $1.28 billion in net incremental annual revenue. Content costs do not scale with a price increase, meaning most of this incremental revenue will flow through to operating income, supporting margin expansion. If NFLX were able to realize $1.28 billion in net incremental revenue as operating income, this alone would result in a 9.6% increase from their 2025 operating income, strengthening the possibility of a beat and raise from NFLX.

WBD Breakup

On February 27, 2026, Netflix conceded to Paramount (PSKY) and walked away from its pursuit of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), collecting a $2.8 billion breakup fee. At the same time, NFLX announced the resumption of its share buyback program. NFLX had previously guided for a 50 basis point operating margin contraction due to anticipated M&A-related expenses. Now that they’re unencumbered by their WBD-related obligations, Netflix can drive free cash flow toward content production and continued share repurchases, increasing earnings per share.

Risks

A key risk to Netflix’s investment thesis is a slowdown in consumer spending. As a consumer discretionary service, streaming subscriptions are typically one of the first items customers cut back on during economic distress. The macroeconomic background is unfavorable for the everyday consumer. Persistent inflation, elevated interest rates, and sustained tariff-driven costs all create pressure for many wallets. When the average gallon of gas is $4.08, consumers are more cognizant of their budget and less likely to divert money toward discretionary items such as streaming subscriptions.

In addition, geopolitical tensions from tariff policy directly affect NFLX. A key strategic priority has been tapping international markets for subscriber growth, such as South Korea, Spain, Brazil, and India. Should trade tensions escalate, NFLX could face margin erosion in international markets due to rising production costs, given that approximately 75% of Netflix’s overall content is produced internationally, as per CNBC.

Conclusion

Netflix is approaching 1Q26 earnings from a position of strength. The recent pullback in share price, driven by broader macroeconomic concerns, presents an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

Looking at consensus estimates, we see that NFLX has received 16 downgrades in the past 90 days with only one upgrade. Estimates stand at $0.76 per share, with revenue estimated to be $12.17 billion. There are three key tailwinds going into the print: an increase in subscription prices, accelerating ad revenue supported by a proprietary ad tech stack, and a $2.8 billion WBD breakup fee.

That being said, there is a material risk to NFLX due to consumer wallets feeling pressure and trade tensions potentially causing margin erosion. However, I believe NFLX will beat earnings estimates and raise full-year guidance with its strong tailwinds. As a result, I rate NFLX a Buy.
